sentences.text: There was over there-- we were very-- my brother and I were only together.
We-- I slept on the bottom bunk.
He slept on the upper bunk.
We had three bunks there.
Three row bunks.
No mattresses-- straw.
No pillows.
Just lay on the straw like an animal.
One time the kapo-- which in the concentration camp police mostly consisting of Jews and sometimes maybe a German.
He picked me for a very-- for a job.
OK, we need six Jews not working.
You're going to be called the Iron Brigade.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
The job consisted to go to the factory-- we were in a camp.
We were in the concentration already.
They took us out under guard, you know-- under machine gun, under short guns, you know?
The SS.
They took us to this particular factory where there was already people-- of non-Jews-- worked there, too, before the war.
And they're still working.
They were manufacturing revolvers, manufacturing bicycles, motorcycles, you know.
Something that the Germans can use.
It was a huge factory.
Before the war, no Jews worked there.
They wouldn't allow Jews to work there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My job consisted-- if somebody-- if somebody's machine breaks down, I had a dolly.
They taught me how to get a dolly, you know.
And bolt-- take off the bolts from the bottom from the floor, put the small machine on the dolly, take the dolly down, and the machine, to the warehouse.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And exchange the machine, put it back, and fasten it on the floor and all.
And let the new people work on the-- this particular machine.
This factory was one of the worst factories you can imagine.
There were three shifts.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] If they caught you sleeping, you didn't get up.
They shot you right at the machine.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If they caught you looking at a part slowly, they caught you-- shot.
If you examined the part for any particular scientific reasons.
No, they got you shot.
If you walked slow, they shot you.
If you walk fast, they shot you.
There was no reason.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was no reason.
There was-- and you were always-- the people who worked there felt their clothes was with oil.
All over, the oil.
Because the oil from the machines, you know.
Because there was grinders there, there were lathes there, there was polishers there.
Different to polish these parts, you know.
No, no.
There was dirts there-- there was-- The machines were working.
And their clothes were very shiny, because the oil completely smothered their clothes, you know.
People were black because the oil on their hands was-- they worked like-- like animals on that machine.
On those machines.
Day in, day out.
Three hours-- three shifts they were there.
But the Poles were there, too.
The Poles.
The Poles were like-- they were like foremans.
They were teaching the Jews how to do it now.
They were nice.
Some of them were nice and some were not.
They were afraid to actually associate with us now.
Me being very gullible, and very immature, and very young.
This happened in 1943.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] End of-- end of '43 though.
I, um-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I, um-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish people-- the other five got together with the underground.
Because we were the only five people, six people, who went with the machine to the warehouse, and no guard was watching us now.
In the whole factory-- always other people were guarded with guns.
If you needed the bathroom, a gun.
Anybody-- we couldn't go away by ourselves, because it was a very highly sensitive, secretive place now.
We were the only six people who went to the warehouse from the second floor down on the steps-- with that machine.
Into the warehouse now.
Pick a new machine.
Sign that out now.
Bring it back to the place now.
Fasten the bottom.
And that's it.
One time-- and they were giving-- and they got-- these other five people got together with the underground.
The underground got together with the Jews.
And they said, look, you go into the warehouse.
Why don't you steal something?
Why don't you bring out the barrel into parts.
The barrel, or firing pin, or a handle from an aeroplane-- from a revolver.
Or from-- bring something up, no?
And in turn, we will give you bread and butter.
You can bring it into the camp now.
And I'm sure there was transactions going on.
I did not know.
Or they were afraid maybe I was too young.
And these were older people.
I mean, they were 18, 19.
I'm sure there was transactions going on between an underground, Polish underground, and them.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My shoes were wooden.
And the-- and I-- and a piece of sliver-- a sliver is a little piece of steel.
When they shave steel, certain things fall away.
Like little-- little-- on the ground.
Shaving steel.
And my shoes were not clogged up.
You know, I used to drag my little wooden shoes.
Clip-- clackers they would call them.
Clackers.
And a piece of sliver, of steel sliver, went into my heel in my left side.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for some reason, right away it swelled up.
And right away I got a big red line between my groin-- all around my groin.
Right away, like a few hours.
Two, three hours.
I got an infection.
Right away, an infection.
Like I was completely disabled.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to my kapo, the man in charge, and I said, look, I cannot do nothing.
What am I going to do?
I cannot work.
I've got a piece of steel in my heel.
Please, what can I do?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So he says, well, let me see.
When the second shift goes back to the camp, I'm going to have you-- I'm going to have you taken back to the camp-- to the concentration camp.
Perhaps one of the guys can take out the piece of sliver from your heel.
And with luck, till tomorrow morning, you can relax now.
And put a little iodine and maybe you can come back tomorrow morning to work.
And so he did.
I went to the hospital.
To the-- a dispensary, it was called.
The army-- the [GERMAN] dispensary, it was called.
I forget that.
And they took out the piece of sliver from my left heel, and they packed it up, and iodine, and a little bit in.
And the line went away.
I felt much better a few hours later, you know?
This wasn't--
I came back in the morning to work.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was just after a huge snow.
Must have been about six inches, maybe 10 inches of snow.
Right near the gate, we went in from the camp into the-- the place of work to that factory.
All the five people were shot and were laying in snow.
They caught them.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And the snow was red, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] like beet.
Like I have never seen in my life now.
And I'm the only one to be here to talk about it.
Because they caught them doing-- taking out these little pieces of parts from the warehouse.
The gave it to the-- they gave it to the underground for exchange for bread.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They were told by the underground-- take out.
Take it out now.
The firing pin, or a barrel, or a handle from a-- or a screw from a revolver now.
Because they could use it, you know, for different parts, you know.
And they would take them out outside the factory, and they can put together a revolver.
I had-- we had direct connection.
But I didn't know.
I mentioned that to you-- that I was very gullible.
I didn't know I had connection.
I was stupid.
I was very naive.
I was very young.
But actually we had the connection.
The other five had the connection with the underground, and they worked together with us in the-- in the factory.
My brother was still in the camp, you know.
He didn't go to work.
He only worked very hard in different-- he did different kind of jobs.
The cleaning, you know, this and that.
My father was still-- still the tailoring and working for the SS.
Doing-- he was doing-- making uniforms in a shop.
Still in Radom.
No.
No, with my brother, we had-- my brother and I were in the same camp in Radom.
In the same concentration camp.
And about-- we-- we, uh-- no, we had contact.
But we never contacted with my father.
No.
My father, we lost contact with him until one time.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was at night.
We use to come up at night, you know, from the camp.
The only-- we didn't wear underwear.
And wear nothing, you know.
And we didn't wash, you know.
We were very dirty.
And-- so the only thing I used to do.
Take off my shirt, my jacket, and I would look for lice.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they used to bite me. had scars all over.
Biting, you know.
And while I was sitting there and looking for lice, the kapo from the block-- from the-- from the barrack.
Since I was a weakling-- young, you know-- he would try to show the heavy guys-- and there were a lot of heavy guys in the-- in the barrack who didn't take his orders.
That I was a weakling.
He walked over to me.
Go to sleep, Steinberg!
Tomorrow's early.
Come and get to sleep!
And he slapped me.
And he slapped me so hard now.
My brother had his-- his can, what he was eating with.
Took the can and he hit the policeman right in his knee.
Don't you ever hit my brother.
He was about 14 or 15 years old.
He says, you do it one more time, I kill you.
That's what he said-- my brother said.
My younger brother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And in the morning-- we went to work in the morning, hoping nothing was going to happen.
And this guy-- we didn't think this Jewish guy will report us to the Germans.
Unfortunately, he reported us to the guy, to the kommandeur, that what we did is a crime.
We hit-- we hit him.
The guy in charge from the Radom camp was a mean SS.
I don't know his rank.
Lieutenant or a captain.
He had just come back from the Russian front.
We used to call him-- he had his behind shot off.
Half  a behind shot off in the front line.
We used to call him, in Jewish, [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But he was a mean man.
He was a man without heart.
[NON-ENGLISH]
[NON-ENGLISH]
[NON-ENGLISH] Yeah.
Yeah.
It means, half-ass.
[LAUGH] Right?
Yeah.
He was known for that.
And we came in back from work.
And we heard near the gate from the-- and I didn't work no more at the Iron Brigade.
I was afraid to tell them I'm the Iron Brigade, too.
So I did some extra work, like cleaning the streets and taking rocks, you know.
Working unloading a truck, the coal, or something.
You know, manual, different works, you know.
Whatever they had people working there, I helped, too.
It was with 20.
I was the 21.
And at that work I was like a helper.
I wasn't-- I was trying to hide myself they shouldn't find out I'm the sixth guy.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they kill.
Because all my friends-- these five people that got killed, that was all my good friends from cheder.
It was so pitiful.
So I came back from the work.
I heard there was screaming, hollering.
Brothers Steinberg! 26203.
Brothers Steinberg!
Number.
Die Bruder Steinberg-- [NON-ENGLISH].
They took us out.
My brother was a very tiny boy.
I was taller.
I was always taller than my brother.
And they took us into the kommandantur.
In the front of the gate was a kommandantur, like a little office.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As soon as I walked in, this [NON-ENGLISH] was there.
This captain-- no, the lieutenant was there.
I think his name was Buchmeier or something.
I don't remember his name.
I don't even want to know it.
I didn't even want to remember his name.
And they told my brother to stay outside.
They said, your time-- don't worry.
I'll talk to you, too, he said.
And he beat me so bad with a rubber hose.
And the rubber hose had on the end a piece of steel, or something wood, or something, you know.
That hit me in my head.
And my back was so sore afterward now.
And, uh-- and after-- I don't know.
After a while, he left me with some scars on my back, on the bottom.
Especially on my leg.
He left with some scars now.
And released me though.
And my brother came in.
Took my brother in.
He beat him with a bamboo stick.
My brother was a young kid, you know.
And until this day-- must have given him 50 to 65 whips on his back for doing that.
He says, you are the smart guy.
You hit the policeman I heard about.
You're the smart guy.
I'll fix you up for that.
And he hit him so hard.
Until this day, my brother's behind is black and blue.
Black, all black.
The blood formed on his behind still black.
We were beaten naked.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one time this is.
So we went back there in the camp and all.
But then my father-- at the same time they took us out from the kommendantur, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after being beaten, they closed up the shops in Radom.
And all the tailors and shoemakers were brought into camp.
And my father saw me and my brother being beaten.
He saw us.
You can imagine how I-- he felt.
Crying.
But we were very close, all three of us.
We missed already-- we missed-- my brother-- younger brother.
We tried to keep a close relationship together.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So, uh-- and he saw us being beaten now.
But, he said, this is not all, so don't worry about it.
My father had a very, very positive attitude in life, you know.
He'd tell, don't worry about it.
We're going to live through it, he says.
We're going to beat them, he said, when we get out.
Don't worry about it though.
Help me?
Help me what?
No.
No.
They were afraid they would be beaten themselves.
Nobody-- what could they do?
You know, we all were in the same shoe.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
I heard-- I don't think so.
They were-- no.
Afterward?
No.
I went to sleep, and I-- that's it.
In the morning, I had to go to work.
I mean, it was something unusually-- the daily routine of life.
You had to accept it, smile about it, and forget about it.
So how long did you remain in Radom?
Well, we remained in Radom until 19-- until one time, a wagon came in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] With a horse.
Let me take this back, please.
There was always something missing in the-- in the mess hall with the kitchen.
There was always 40 breads missing, 60 breads missing, 10 breads missing.
And they used to accuse the Jews of stealing.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then there was wiring missing.
There was water missing.
There was a lot of things missing.
There was pipe missing-- pipes, you know?
There were people missing.
When they took the appel, when they counted us, you know-- there was always people missing.
They couldn't figure out.
The Germans just couldn't figure out who-- what it is.
They went through.
They smelled, they-- they talked to-- they checked with lights and everything.
They couldn't figure out.
No.
But when they built Radom camp, there was some engineers.
They were involved.
And they-- and they did something.
They, uh-- that certain parts of the floor from the kitchen they left exposed, not nailed.
I never knew that either.
I found it out when I was in camps.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So this one time, we didn't know about it.
We figured, well listen, we didn't worry about if somebody.
We were just-- they punished us for missing.
They said, I'm sure some of you stole, you know.
And we're going to punish you for that now.
So they hit somebody.
They made us stay about three, four hours appel outside for punishment.
Being beaten.
Somebody beaten.
We used to always congregate in a circle. 10 to a circle, five to a circle.
In the middle, they had a pulpit.
And they'd put somebody over-- bend over.
And they used to beat them.
I don't know.
Sometimes, I wonder how we all survived.
But you know, this is the truth.
And a wagon came in.
That wagon had a big, huge barrel.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That barrel picked up the waste from the toilet.
The toilet was about-- about 200 feet away from the barracks.
It was a straight piece of board with holes.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was the most awful thing.
And it was like a punishment.
A big, huge hole.
If you were-- if somebody had a large-- a small behind, easy to fall into it.
It was impossible to hold on.
They thought it was very funny, the Germans.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And all the time, there was waste in between the sides, you know.
There was urine in the front, you know.
But, I mean, when you have to, you have to, you know.
But--
The truck-- its wheel caved in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The left wheel caved in.
The first thing we thought, maybe we got into quicksand because it was sand.
It was [NON-ENGLISH].
We couldn't understand what's going on.
They called some German police, and they called the SS, you know.
They brought in dogs.
They smelled the place.
And they give us an order to dig.
They took about, I don't know, a few people there.
And they started digging the hole.
And what did they find?
They found a tremendous [NON-ENGLISH]-- tunnel.
Genuine, scientifically designed.
With lights, with water, with bread, with a table.
They were three, or four-- or I don't know-- five feet away from the gate.
From the fence to get out.
And they were in certain spots, the tunnel was lower.
But they probably didn't know.
But in certain spots, it was higher to the ground.
That's when they were discovered, because it was too close to the ground.
To the--
And they took out these people.
I shall never-- I'm 70 years old right now.
I should never in my life forget when they brought these people out.
Because we all stayed in appel to watch these people in a circle.
There was a lady there.
She was white as a ghost.
I have never seen the color white as white.
She had red hair.
The reason she was so white, I'm sure, because she never saw sunshine.
She was in the dark all her life.
And all these people were white.
They brought them out, you know.
They put them in the middle of the circle.
They undressed them.
And they beat them and whipped them so hard.
But they didn't shoot them.
Because they admired their-- their ingenuity.
Their smart ways how they built it.
They said, my golly, we have never seen that.
They were hiding.
They were trying to get out.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were hiding in the tunnel.
There were about-- I don't know.
Altogether, there was a few people there.
I couldn't tell you the amount now because-- but I know they were pulling out people.
I don't know.
Nine, or 10, or 11.
I don't know exactly.
But there was quite a few people there.
The one who were missing to the appel before were there.
Yeah, I remember.
One was a girl I went to shul with them.
In fact, this was his sister.
His father was a baker.
He had a bakery.
I think it was a-- he was making, uh-- ciastos.
Which means like a cakes.
[NON-ENGLISH]?
Little cakes.
Like, you know, little cakes, yeah.
I don't know.
I don't know.
I, uh-- no, I lived in a very selfish way.
I didn't worry about anybody else.
Only myself.
I don't know.
And after the war, I don't know.
I never paid attention to it.
I'm sure they never survived.
Because we went-- because this life started-- started bad.
From this is how it started very bad.
[LAUGH]
Well, we were taken out once in the morning-- this camp.
The Russians were advancing already.
They were already in a city called Lublin or near Lublin.
I can't remember.
And I was already-- this was in '40-- end of '43.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I want to stress the fact that Radom ammunition factory was one of the worst factory.
One of the-- one of the worst factory that Jews can encumber-- could live through.
It was owned by a company called Steyr-Daimler and Puch.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
After the war, they denied it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Steyr-Daimler is involved with Mercedes.
But Jews buy Mercedes, no?
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The only thing that's missing is Benz.
[LAUGH]
Then in the morning, you know, they got a-- people died every day.
People were dying, you know, in that camp.
That Radom camp.
Then they took us on a death march.
We marched 100 or 100 something kilometers to another city.
Day and night for three or four nights we marched.
Marched, day and night.
Probably in the, uh-- it wasn't the fall.
In between.
Like summer.
Because we were always very thirsty, you know?
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my brother, and I, and my father we were on the same line walking.
I think there were 10 in a line.
We went through the city.
We went through it, Radom.
We didn't walk fast.
We walked very slow.
They made us-- they made us sing songs, you know.
They made us-- being jokey, you know?
Like you know, being silly.
They made a fun of us.
And there were times they came over to us.
Are you tired?
If somebody said yes-- come on, go in the back.
Go in the wagon now.
We will take you.
We will ride you.
But in the meantime, the wagon went in the woods, you know.
And they  shot them right there.
Did the SS walk with you?
They were on the horses.
They were on-- they were on-- on these wagons with the horse.
And some of them walked.
Because there were so many SS.
They were watching these Jews like we would be the most precious things in the world, you know.
We went-- day and night, we went, you know-- to the city of Tomaszow.
On the way, going up there, I had this terrible stomach ache.
I stayed with my father.
We had to-- we locked ourself in.
We had-- the first guy from this one and this one was responsible the middle one.
We had locked ourselves in.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And I says, Dad, Pappy.
I said, I have a terrible stomach.
What am I going to do?
He said, well-- he slapped me.
I'll slap you, he says.
Now you can forget about this.
Make it while you walk.
Since I didn't wear no underwear, it just flowed through.
We walked, and walked, and walked with no food.
When we went by near a place of puddled water, people dropped down and drank dirty water.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then they were shot for doing that.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then we stopped, and they gave us some soup.
They gave us some water soup, you know.
And we ate something, you know.
And then from that trip, from that group-- from that group we were marching-- I think maybe a quarter was shot.
Maybe even more than a quarter was shot, you know.
